pattern: A bounded max-heap that only ever holds the k smallest distances seen so far reports the current k-th nearest obstacle in logarithmic time per query, without ever re-sorting the whole history.
hints: You only ever care about the k smallest distances built up so far, not the full history, so keep a structure that tracks just those k values.
A max-heap capped at size k works: once it holds k distances, its top is the current k-th nearest, and a new obstacle only matters if it beats that top.
Before the heap reaches size k there simply aren't enough obstacles yet, so the answer for that query is -1.

edge_inputs.description: fewer than k obstacles at first, then enough
k equals one, tracking the single nearest obstacle
a single query with k equal to one
k larger than the number of queries never reaches an answer
two obstacles land at exactly the same distance from the origin
negative coordinates must contribute their absolute distance
a later obstacle closer than all previous ones displaces the current k-th nearest

stress.description: random unique query coordinates across the full range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    seen = set()
    queries = []
    while len(queries) < size:
        x = rng.randint(-1000000000, 1000000000)
        y = rng.randint(-1000000000, 1000000000)
        if (x, y) in seen:
            continue
        seen.add((x, y))
        queries.append([x, y])
    k = rng.randint(1, max(1, size // 2))
    return [queries, k]
stress.note: obstacle coordinates are always unique, as the problem guarantees

oracle_python:
class Solution:
    def resultsArray(self, queries, k):
        # Independent implementation that re-sorts every obstacle seen so far
        # after each query, rather than maintaining a bounded heap.
        obstacles = []
        res = []
        for x, y in queries:
            obstacles.append(abs(x) + abs(y))
            s = sorted(obstacles)
            res.append(s[k - 1] if len(s) >= k else -1)
        return res
